title: Interactions API: our primary interface for Gemini models and agents
gist: Google DeepMind has moved its Interactions API to general availability and declared it the primary, default interface for Gemini models and agents, signaling a strategic pivot from the stateless generateContent API toward stateful, agent-first workflows where frontier long-running and agentic capabilities will increasingly ship exclusively on the new API.

insights.claim: Google has made its Interactions API the primary and default interface for all interaction with Gemini models and agents, replacing the legacy generateContent API as the recommended path for new development.
insights.speaker: Ali Çevik
insights.so_what: Signals Google is consolidating its developer surface around an agent-native API; partners and tooling built on generateContent should plan migration to stay on the frontier.
insights.quote: Today we're announcing that the Interactions API has reached general availability and is now our primary API for interacting with Gemini models and agents.
insights.locator: Today we're announcing that the Interactions API

insights.claim: Google expects frontier capabilities for long-running models and agents to increasingly land exclusively on the Interactions API rather than the legacy generateContent API, because the new API is designed from the ground up for stateful, agentic workflows.
insights.speaker: Ali Çevik
insights.so_what: Developers and vendors relying solely on generateContent risk being cut off from new agentic features; the moat is shifting to stateful APIs.
insights.quote: we expect frontier capabilities for long-running models and agents to increasingly land exclusively on the Interactions API. This is because it is designed from the ground up for stateful, agentic workflows.
insights.locator: While the legacy generateContent API remains fully supported

insights.claim: Google is working with ecosystem partners to make the Interactions API the default interface across third-party SDKs and libraries, and it is already the default for Google AI Studio, the Gemini API, and all documentation.
insights.speaker: Ali Çevik
insights.so_what: A coordinated default-switch across docs and 3P tooling accelerates lock-in to Google's agent stack; watch adoption velocity among integration partners.
insights.quote: we are working with ecosystem partners to make it the default interface across 3P SDKs and Libraries.
insights.locator: All of our documentation now defaults to Interactions API

insights.claim: The legacy generateContent API will remain fully supported and continue to receive new mainline Gemini models for the foreseeable future, with a published migration guide mapping every field to the new schema.
insights.speaker: Ali Çevik
insights.so_what: Backward compatibility is preserved, lowering migration urgency for basic inference but not for agentic features.
insights.quote: the legacy generateContent API remains fully supported and will continue to receive new mainline Gemini models for the foreseeable future
insights.locator: While the legacy generateContent API remains fully supported

insights.claim: A single Interactions API call provisions a remote Linux sandbox where a Gemini agent can reason, execute code, browse the web, and manage files, with the Antigravity agent shipping as the default and support for custom agents defined with instructions, skills, and data sources.
insights.speaker: Ali Çevik
insights.so_what: Google is bundling compute and sandboxed agent runtime into the API itself, competing directly with agent-infra vendors; watch margin impact and displacement of third-party sandbox providers.
insights.quote: A single API call provisions a remote Linux sandbox where an agent can reason, execute code, browse the web and manage files.
insights.locator: Managed Agents: A single API call

insights.claim: The Interactions API supports background execution by setting background=True on any call, running the interaction asynchronously on the server with server-side state and 55-day retention of past interactions on the paid tier.
insights.speaker: Ali Çevik
insights.so_what: Server-side state plus async execution moves orchestration burden off the client, raising switching costs and enabling long-running agent products.
insights.quote: Past interactions are retrievable with 55-day retention on the paid tier.

insights.claim: The Interactions API replaces the old role-based structure with a simplified schema in which every action (user_input, thought, function_call, model_output, etc.) is its own typed step.
insights.speaker: Ali Çevik
insights.so_what: A typed-step schema is purpose-built for agent traces and tool calls; expect downstream tooling (observability, eval) to standardize around this shape.
insights.quote: Simplified schema where every action (user_input, thought, function_call, model_output, etc.) is its own typed step, replacing the old role structure.

insights.claim: The Interactions API lets developers mix built-in tools such as Google Search and Google Maps with their own functions in a single request, and tool results can now return images alongside text.
insights.speaker: Ali Çevik
insights.so_what: Native bundling of Google's first-party data tools (Search, Maps) into agent calls leverages Google's distribution moat that rival model providers lack.
insights.quote: Mix built-in tools , such as Google Search, Google Maps with your own functions in one request. Tool results can now return images alongside text.

insights.claim: Google's Deep Research now offers two agent versions optimized for speed versus depth, plus collaborative planning, native charts and infographics, and multimodal grounding with images, PDFs and audio.
insights.speaker: Ali Çevik
insights.so_what: A speed/depth tiering of research agents mirrors a maturing product line; multimodal grounding broadens the addressable knowledge-work use cases.
insights.quote: Two new agent versions (speed vs. depth), collaborative planning, native charts and infographics, and multimodal grounding with images, PDFs and audio.

insights.claim: The Interactions API exposes named media-generation models including Nano Banana 2 for image generation with Google Image Search grounding, Lyria 3 for music, and multi-speaker TTS for expressive speech.
insights.speaker: Ali Çevik
insights.so_what: Identifies Google's current-gen media model stack (image/music/speech) consolidated behind one API; useful for tracking Google's multimodal generation roadmap.
insights.quote: Image generation with Nano Banana 2 and Google Image Search grounding, music with Lyria 3, and expressive speech with multi-speaker TTS.

insights.claim: Google offers Flex and Priority tiers on the Interactions API to optimize for cost or latency, with the Flex tier providing a 50% cost reduction.
insights.speaker: Ali Çevik
insights.so_what: A 50% Flex discount signals aggressive pricing to win latency-tolerant agent/batch workloads; pressures competitors' per-token economics.
insights.quote: Flex and Priority tiers let you optimize for cost or latency (Flex offers 50% cost reduction).

insights.claim: Google asserts that most developers are now using coding agents such as Antigravity to build applications, and has shipped a gemini-interactions-api Skill that injects best-practice API patterns directly into a coding agent's context.
insights.speaker: Ali Çevik
insights.so_what: Google is designing its API distribution for agent-built software rather than human developers; the 'Skill' injection pattern is a new channel for steering agent-generated code toward a vendor's stack.
insights.quote: Most developers are now using coding agents (such as Antigravity) to build applications.

insights.claim: The Interactions API is available via Python and JavaScript SDKs and already has integrations with partners LiteLLM, Eigent and Agno.
insights.speaker: Ali Çevik
insights.so_what: Named integration partners (LiteLLM, Eigent, Agno) mark Google's beachhead in the agent-framework ecosystem; watch whether competing model routers adopt the new schema as default.
insights.quote: If you're already building with one of our supported partners, LiteLLM, Eigent or Agno, you can start using their Interactions API integrations today.

insights.claim: The Interactions API launched in public beta in December 2025 and reached general availability by June 2026, gaining a stable schema plus new capabilities including Managed Agents, background execution, and Gemini Omni (coming soon).
insights.speaker: Ali Çevik
insights.so_what: A roughly six-month beta-to-GA timeline indicates fast iteration; the forthcoming Gemini Omni is a roadmap item to watch.
insights.quote: We launched its public beta in December 2025, and it has quickly become developers’ favorite way to build applications with Gemini.
insights.locator: Today we're announcing that the Interactions API
